message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  In this paper, we analyze the real-time infection data of COVID-19 epidemic for 21 nations up to June 30, 2020. For most of these nations, the total number of infected individuals exhibits a succession of exponential growth and power-law growth before the flattening of the curve. In particular, we find a universal
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20091389v3_inline1.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  growth before they reach saturation. However, at present, India, which has
                  <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) ~
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                  , and Russia and Brazil, which have
                  <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) ~
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  , are yet to flatten their curves. Thus, the polynomials of the
                  <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) curves provide valuable information on the stage of the epidemic evolution, thus on the life cycle of COVID-19 pandemic. Besides these detailed analyses, we compare the predictions of an extended SEIR model and a delay differential equation-based model with the reported infection data and observed good agreement among them, including the
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20091389v3_inline2.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  behaviour. We argue that the power laws in the epidemic curves may be due to lockdowns.
                </jats:p>
title: Evolution of COVID-19 pandemic: Power-law growth and saturation
